content.full_text:
stephanie Atigh 
City Attorney 
City of Salinas 
200 Lincoln Avenue 
Salinas, CA 
93901 
Dear Ms. Atigh: 
January 10, 1990 
Re: 
Your Request for Confirmation of 
Telephone Advice 
Our File No. A-89-712 
We have received your letter dated December 26, 1989 seeking 
written confirmation of the telephone advice provided to you by 
this agency on December 21, 1989 regarding the responsibilities of 
a member of the Salinas City Council under the conflict-of-in-
terest provisions of the Political Reform Act (lithe Act,,).1 
Based on the facts you provided in your telephone inquiry and 
reiterated in your letter, your letter substantially reflects the 
tenor of the advice given to you, although some clarification is 
necessary. 
You were advised that the councilmember's participation in 
decisions concerning the construction of a public parking garage 
at this time may constitute a conflict of interest for the coun-
cilmember under the Act. 
The conflict may be present because: (1) 
the decision concerning the public parking garage could have a 
foreseeable material financial effect on a developer whose obliga-
tions under a use permit to provide sixty-one parking spaces could 
be extinguished by the decisions, and (2) the developer is a 
source of income to the councilmember. 
(Section 87103(c).) 
You were further advised that decisions concerning the public 
parking garage that did not impact the developer's existing obli-
gations under the land use permit concerning the sixty-one parking 
spaces would not trigger similar conflict issues, because such 
1 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 
18000, 
seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • 
Sacramento CA 95804~0807 .. (916) 322-=;660 


File No. A-89-712 
Page 2 
decisions which had no foreseeable effect on the developer's obli-
gation under the land use permit would not require disqualifica-
tion. 
Based on this analysis, you were advised that the councilmem-
ber should initially abstain from decisions concerning the parking 
garage until the issue progressed and a more clear idea of the 
project was developed. 
Whether the councilmember may participate 
in subsequent decisions concerning the parking garage would depend 
on the impact of the decisions on the developer's obligations un-
der the land use permit. 
However, you were not advised with respect to any matter con-
cerning a council decision "to deny the developer the use of the 
parking garage to offset the planning unit development permit com-
mercial parking requirement." 
You had not inquired about, and 
thus were not advised with respect to, any such matter: such a 
council decision raises different issues and requires different 
analysis. 
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you 
requested. 
If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:JSR:plh 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: Jonat a~~~ 
Counsel, Legal Division 


city of salinas 
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY' 200 lincoln Avenue· Salinas, Call1ofrHi! 9]901 • (408) 7S8,7256 
December 26, 1989 
John Rothman 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 "J" Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
SUBJECT: 
CONFIRMATION OF TELEPHONE ADVICE HE CONFLICT 
Dear Mr. Rothman: 
Please consider this letter a request for written confirmation of 
telephone advice received on December 21, 1989 regarding a poten-
tial conflict with a City Councilmember. 
The facts upon which you based your verbal advice are as follows: 
Councilmember is an attorney representing a developer who has re-
ceived a discretionary land use entitlement (Planned Unit Devel-
opment Permit). 
The permit requires the commercial phase of the 
development to provide 61 parking spaces, and it specifies that 
"these spaces may be provided in a future parking district". 
The 
Councilmember has not and does not represent the developer in 
this land use matter. 
The October 17 earthquake caused significant damage to City 
buildings in the 100 block of Main Street which is one block away 
from the proposed PUD development. 
Several buildings were demol-
ished in this 100 block, and the City anticipates receiving FEMA 
monies as reimbursement. 
One of the suggestions for the 
uses of 
these monies is to construct a public parking garage on City 
property in the 100 block. 
It is not known at this time if the FEMA money will cover the 
entire cost of the garage or merely a portion of the cost since 
cost will depend on size and configuration. 
It is possible that 
FEMA money will pay for 
portion of the structure with an as-
sessment district paying for the remainder of the construction as 
well as maintenance costs. 
FEMA does not allow the City to seek 
duplicate reimbursement for an 
FEMA moni s received. 


Mr. Joh 
Rothman 
December 26, 1989 
Pag( 
2 
there is d conflict for the Counci Imember to takv part in a 
decision to issue the RFP and in other decisions involving this 
parking garage. 
Thank you for your cooperation and assistance In ttlis request. 
Sincerely,

California

Practices Commission 
stephanie Atigh 
city Attorney 
200 Lincoln Avenue 
Salinas, CA 
93901 
Dear Ms. Atigh: 
January 8, 1990 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-712 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on December 26, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact Jonathan Rothman an attorney in the Legal 
Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
Very truly yours, 
I. 
I 
I

\' 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel

sections.question: The facts upon which you based your verbal advice are as follows: Councilmember is an attorney representing a developer who has received a discretionary land use entitlement (Planned Unit Development Permit). The permit requires the commercial phase of the development to provide 61 parking spaces, and it specifies that 'these spaces may be provided in a future parking district'. The Councilmember has not and does not represent the developer in this land use matter. The October 17 earthquake caused significant damage to City buildings in the 100 block of Main Street which is one block away from the proposed PUD development. Several buildings were demolished in this 100 block, and the City anticipates receiving FEMA monies as reimbursement. One of the suggestions for the uses of these monies is to construct a public parking garage on City property in the 100 block. It is not known at this time if the FEMA money will cover the entire cost of the garage or merely a portion of the cost since cost will depend on size and configuration. It is possible that FEMA money will pay for portion of the structure with an assessment district paying for the remainder of the construction as well as maintenance costs. FEMA does not allow the City to seek duplicate reimbursement for FEMA monies received. Is there a conflict for the Councilmember to take part in a decision to issue the RFP and in other decisions involving this parking garage?
sections.conclusion: You were advised that the councilmember's participation in decisions concerning the construction of a public parking garage at this time may constitute a conflict of interest for the councilmember under the Act. The conflict may be present because: (1) the decision concerning the public parking garage could have a foreseeable material financial effect on a developer whose obligations under a use permit to provide sixty-one parking spaces could be extinguished by the decisions, and (2) the developer is a source of income to the councilmember. You were further advised that decisions concerning the public parking garage that did not impact the developer's existing obligations under the land use permit concerning the sixty-one parking spaces would not trigger similar conflict issues. Based on this analysis, the councilmember should initially abstain from decisions concerning the parking garage until the issue progressed and a more clear idea of the project was developed. Whether the councilmember may participate in subsequent decisions concerning the parking garage would depend on the impact of the decisions on the developer's obligations under the land use permit.
sections.analysis:
. 
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you 
requested.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a City Councilmember who represents a developer in other matters have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if the councilmember participates in decisions regarding a public parking garage that could affect the developer's obligation to provide parking spaces under an existing land use permit?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilmember has a potential conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if participating in parking garage decisions that could affect the developer's existing parking obligations. The councilmember should initially abstain, and may only participate in subsequent decisions if those decisions do not impact the developer's obligations under the existing land use permit.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document consists of three letters: (1) incoming request from City Attorney Atigh dated 12/26/1989 seeking confirmation of telephone advice; (2) FPPC intake letter dated 1/8/1990; and (3) FPPC substantive response letter dated 1/10/1990. The substantive response letter is the main advice letter. OCR quality is good. Document clearly discusses conflict of interest analysis under Government Code section 87103(c). FPPC notes it did not advise on the specific matter of denying developer use of the garage.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The facts upon which you based your verbal advice are as follows: Councilmember is an attorney representing a developer who has received a discretionary land use entitlement (Planned Unit Development Permit). The permit requires the commercial phase of the development to provide 61 parking spaces, and it specifies that 'these spaces may be provided in a future parking district'. The Councilmember has not and does not represent the developer in this land use matter. The October 17 earthquake caused significant damage to City buildings in the 100 block of Main Street which is one block away from the proposed PUD development. Several buildings were demolished in this 100 block, and the City anticipates receiving FEMA monies as reimbursement. One of the suggestions for the uses of these monies is to construct a public parking garage on City property in the 100 block. It is not known at this time if the FEMA money will cover the entire cost of the garage or merely a portion of the cost since cost will depend on size and configuration. It is possible that FEMA money will pay for portion of the structure with an assessment district paying for the remainder of the construction as well as maintenance costs. FEMA does not allow the City to seek duplicate reimbursement for FEMA monies received. Is there a conflict for the Councilmember to take part in a decision to issue the RFP and in other decisions involving this parking garage?

CONCLUSION: You were advised that the councilmember's participation in decisions concerning the construction of a public parking garage at this time may constitute a conflict of interest for the councilmember under the Act. The conflict may be present because: (1) the decision concerning the public parking garage could have a foreseeable material financial effect on a developer whose obligations under a use permit to provide sixty-one parking spaces could be extinguished by the decisions, and (2) the developer is a source of income to the councilmember. You were further advised that decisions concerning the public parking garage that did not impact the developer's existing obligations under the land use permit concerning the sixty-one parking spaces would not trigger similar conflict issues. Based on this analysis, the councilmember should initially abstain from decisions concerning the parking garage until the issue progressed and a more clear idea of the project was developed. Whether the councilmember may participate in subsequent decisions concerning the parking garage would depend on the impact of the decisions on the developer's obligations under the land use permit.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides written confirmation of prior telephone advice regarding a City Councilmember's conflict of interest in decisions about a public parking garage that could affect a developer's obligations, clarifying that the conflict depends on whether decisions impact the developer's land use permit obligations and that the councilmember should initially abstain.
